evidence_quotes.quote: in the case of video games.
license or subscription that can be revoked at any moment for any reason the developers or platform deem sufficient.
end of life plan so that the consumers aren't being tricked into having just a license or subscription

paragraphs.text: I've been following the development of the Stop Killing Games Initiative for a couple of months now and I can't just sit on the sidelines anymore. I know this whole wave of concerned citizens talking about their games might be taken out of context by opposing parties, especially with how the world is shaping in our current times, but that just goes to prove how badly things need to change. A prime example that I'd like to point out is EVE Valkyrie, developed and killed by CCP Games. This might not be the best example but it comes from a company that is known for their game EVE Online and having too many short lived projects, with EVE Valkyrie being one of them. Both games being catered to an online player base, weren't thought out to be playable for decades to come, even with EVE Online's history. If the company no longer profits or no longer feels like the projected sales are worth it, the game dies as fast as legally possible. Sometimes, legality is not even considered or a concern if the big number doesn't get bigger for the investors and people at the top. I would also like to share something from the physical world, not just the digital one. Currently, I'm being bombarded by ads to board games due to my interest in a couple of new ones that I've ended up buying. The ads consist of all sorts of different board games with their crowdfunding campaigns, such as gamefound or kickstarter. These campaigns are based on the public interest. If the campaign gets enough funds, the board games go further into development, pre-production, printing, shipping, etc. The key factor here is the public interest. The developers need to have enough of a coherent idea and design to attract enough public interest in order for it to take shape further along the development process into something that they can deliver. Sometimes that ends up being a disappointment, other times it leads to a gem of a product that has way more demand than supply. In both outcomes, there's a product that has to be delivered, which can't be all of a sudden taken back or cancelled without a refund. This is a very important right that the consumers have in this example AND it should be a right in the case of video games. If the consumers buy a game, even if it's designed to mainly be played online, that game needs to have an end of life plan so that the consumers aren't being tricked into having just a license or subscription that can be revoked at any moment for any reason the developers or platform deem sufficient. The consumers need to be able to enjoy/play that game even after the company has deemed it unprofitable or unsuccessful OR they have to refund the money since their product is no longer working/available to the consumers. Other examples that I could give are best kept to a few business/market strategies, like planned or unplanned obsolescence, that can be used in almost any market or industry, not just games. Home appliances, cars, phones, laptops, etc.
